1: “Son of man, stand on your feet” (ben-adam, amad al-ragleykha) — the command to rise after prostration is the standard commissioning gesture in prophetic and angelic encounter literature. Moses hid his face at the burning bush (Exodus 3:6); Isaiah was undone by the vision (Isaiah 6:5); Daniel fell prostrate at every heavenly encounter. The divine command to stand signals that the encounter is purposive — not for contemplation but for commission. Ben-adam (son of man) is Ezekiel's characteristic address, occurring 93 times in the book — more than in the entire rest of the OT combined. It simultaneously emphasizes human creaturely finitude (as opposed to the divine glory just witnessed) and authorizes the prophet as YHWH's representative.
2: The Spirit entering the prophet to enable him to stand (vatavo bi ruach vaatamideni al-raglai) is the pneumatological dimension of prophetic commissioning. In OT theology the Spirit comes upon prophets to empower speech and action (1 Samuel 10:6; Isaiah 61:1; Joel 2:28). Here the Spirit's first action is more basic: to enable the prophet to stand upright before the divine presence. The Spirit's empowerment for prophetic mission begins with the most elemental act — standing — before proceeding to speaking.
3: “To nations that have rebelled against me” — the unusual plural “nations” (goyim) for Israel/Judah suggests that Israel's covenant-breach has made them functionally indistinguishable from the pagan nations in terms of their rebellious relationship to YHWH. The same rhetorical move appears in Ezekiel 25:8 (Moab says Israel is “like all the nations” — which is both the insult and the accurate diagnosis). “They and their ancestors have transgressed against me right up to this day” establishes that the exile is not a surprising rupture but the climax of a continuous pattern — a theme developed extensively in Ezekiel 20's historical review.
4: “Brazen-faced and stubborn-hearted” (chazaqey-panim uqeshey-lev) — the face-heart combination is physiognomic language common to wisdom literature: the face reveals the inner disposition, and a hardened face signals a hardened will. In ANE physiognomics (a developed discipline in Mesopotamia), facial features were correlated with character traits and destiny. Ezekiel uses this vocabulary to diagnose Israel's covenant condition: not merely behavioral disobedience but a structural orientation of will away from YHWH. The formula “Thus says the Lord GOD” (ko amar Adonai YHWH) appears over 200 times in Ezekiel — the royal messenger formula of the ancient Near Eastern diplomatic tradition, claiming the authority of the highest suzerain.
5: “Whether they listen or refuse — for they are a rebellious house — they will know that a prophet has been among them.” The prophetic commission is explicitly outcome-independent: success is not measured by audience response but by faithful delivery. This counters the common ancient assumption (and modern one) that a prophet's validity is proven by popular acceptance. The formula “rebellious house” (beit hameri) is Ezekiel's label for his audience throughout the book (Ezekiel 2:5, 6, 7, 8; Ezekiel 3:9, 26, 27; Ezekiel 12:2, 3, 9, 25; Ezekiel 17:12; Ezekiel 24:3) — it frames every subsequent interaction as communication with a people whose disposition is already known to be hostile.
6: “Briers and thorns and scorpions” — the thorn-and-thistle imagery recalls the curse of Genesis 3:18 (the ground producing thorns after the fall) and is used elsewhere for hostile social environments (Micah 7:4; Isaiah 9:18). Scorpions represent lethal danger lurking in the territory one must traverse — a vivid image for the prophetic ministry among hostile hearers. The triple prohibition of fear (“do not be afraid... or be terrified... Do not fear... or be dismayed”) mirrors Joshua's commissioning formula (Joshua 1:6-9) — Ezekiel is sent on a mission as difficult as entering Canaan, and requires the same divine assurance.
7: “You shall speak my words to them, whether they listen or not, for they are utterly rebellious” (ki meri hemah). The unconditional prophetic commission — speak regardless of response — is theologically important: it means Ezekiel's words are not conditional on audience readiness but on divine mandate. This principle is developed in Ezekiel 3's watchman theology: the prophet's responsibility is discharge of the message, not guaranteeing its reception. The word “utterly rebellious” is the superlative of meri (rebellion), suggesting maximum resistance.
8: “Do not be like that rebellious house — open your mouth and eat what I give you.” The command to eat the scroll positions the prophetic word as food — a metaphor with deep roots in ANE royal-court tradition. Scribes who served the king literally “ate from the king's table,” and receiving the king's word was described in digestive terms. More directly, Jeremiah had said “your words were found and I ate them; your words became to me a joy and the delight of my heart” (Jeremiah 15:16). Ezekiel is commanded to do literally what Jeremiah described metaphorically.
9: The hand extended with the scroll — a visual echo of hands that appear in prophetic visions (Isaiah 6:6-7, where a seraph takes a coal with tongs; Daniel 10:18, where a hand touches Daniel). The scroll written on both sides (panim veachor) is unusual because ancient scrolls were typically written on one side only (the papyrus or parchment's smooth inner surface). Writing on the reverse (verso) was uncommon and associated with overflow — too much content for one side. The content — lamentations, mourning, woe (qinah vahaggeh vahahi) — covers the three Hebrew categories of grief literature.
10: The three words on the scroll — “lamentations, mourning, and woe” (qinah vahaggeh vahahi) — constitute Ezekiel's entire prophetic assignment in Ezekiel 1-24: the announcement of judgment on Jerusalem and Judah. Qinah is the dirge-form of Hebrew poetry (typically 3+2 stress pattern, used in Lamentations); haggeh is groaning or moaning; ahi is the exclamation of distress. Together they are the sounds of a community in mourning — the sounds of 586 BCE being anticipated by the prophet in 593 BCE. The scroll pre-inscribes the future: the judgment is already written before Ezekiel speaks a word.
